* identify the main chemical elements in the human body;
* describe and draw basic atomic structure;
* identify the differences between atoms, ions, molecules and compounds;
* distinguish the difference between ionic and covalent bonds;
* define a chemical reaction and identify the difference between an endergonic and exergonic reaction;
* define energy and the units of energy;
* describe the role of ATP within the cell;
* describe the common forms of chemical reactions including synthesis, decomposition, reversible, exchange, phosphorylation/dephosphorylation and oxidation/reduction reactions;
* outline the functions of water and the chemical property underpinning water’s ability to act as a solvent;
* define the terms mole, molar and molarity;
* define the terms acid, base and salt;
* draw and define the pH scale and describe what is meant by the terms acidic and alkaline;
* describe basic cell structure with particular emphasis to the plasma membrane, nucleus, cytoplasm and mitochondria.

---

## Definition

A foundational chapter outlining the basic chemistry and cell biology required to understand biochemistry for sport and exercise. It covers the chemical elements of the human body, atomic structure, the distinctions between atoms, ions, molecules and compounds, and the nature of chemical reactions and energy transfer within cells.

## Core Principles

- The human body is composed of a small set of main chemical elements, organised into atoms with a defined basic atomic structure.
- Atoms, ions, molecules and compounds are distinct entities; chemical bonds between atoms can be ionic or covalent, with different properties.
- A chemical reaction involves the transformation of reactants into products; reactions are classified energetically as endergonic (energy-absorbing) or exergonic (energy-releasing).
- Energy is the capacity to do work and is quantified in defined units of energy.
- ATP serves as the central energy currency within the cell, coupling exergonic and endergonic processes.
- Common reaction types include synthesis, decomposition, reversible, exchange, phosphorylation/dephosphorylation, and oxidation/reduction reactions.
- Water's polarity and hydrogen-bonding capacity underpin its function as a biological solvent and its other physiological roles.
- Acids, bases and salts are defined by their behaviour in solution, and the pH scale quantifies acidity and alkalinity.
- Basic cell structure includes the plasma membrane, nucleus, cytoplasm and mitochondria, each with distinct biochemical roles.

## Sub-concepts

- Main chemical elements of the human body

- Atoms, ions, molecules and compounds

- Chemical reactions: endergonic vs exergonic
- Energy and units of energy

- Reaction classes: synthesis, decomposition, reversible, exchange, phosphorylation/dephosphorylation, oxidation/reduction
- Water as a solvent; functions of water
- Mole, molar and molarity
- Acid, base, salt
- pH scale; acidic and alkaline
- Plasma membrane, nucleus, cytoplasm, mitochondria

## Field-level Controversies

## Practical Applications

- Provides the chemical and cellular vocabulary required to interpret metabolic pathways relevant to sport and exercise.
- Quantitative concepts (mole, molar, molarity; units of energy) underpin laboratory measurements and dietary/energy calculations in exercise science.
- Understanding ATP and oxidation/reduction reactions is foundational for analysing energy systems used during physical activity.
- pH and acid/base concepts inform the interpretation of exercise-induced acidosis and buffering.

## Related Concepts

- ATP and cellular energy metabolism
- Oxidation–reduction reactions
- Acid–base balance and pH regulation
- Cell structure and organelle function
- Water and solvent chemistry in physiology
